docs.title: Env-Doctor
docs.text:
I just wasted 3 hours fighting CUDA errors that broke my GPU environment! Been there?

"Why does my PyTorch crash with CUDA errors when I just installed it?"

Because your driver supports CUDA 11.8, but pip install torch gave you CUDA 12.4 wheels.

Env-Doctor diagnoses and fixes the #1 frustration in GPU computing: mismatched CUDA versions between your NVIDIA driver, system toolkit (nvcc compiler), cuDNN, and Python libraries.

It takes 5 seconds to find out if your environment is broken — and exactly how to fix it.

docs.title: Features
docs.text: Feature What It Does One-Command Diagnosis Instantly check compatibility between GPU Driver → CUDA Toolkit → cuDNN → PyTorch/TensorFlow/JAX Deep CUDA Analysis Reveals multiple installations, PATH issues, environment misconfigurations Compilation Guard Warns if system nvcc doesn't match PyTorch's CUDA — preventing flash-attention build failures WSL2 GPU Support Detects WSL1/WSL2 environments, validates GPU forwarding, catches common driver conflicts for WSL2 Safe Install Commands Prescribes the exact pip install command that works with YOUR driver Container Validation Catches GPU config errors in Dockerfiles and docker-compose with DB-driven recommendations AI Model Compatibility Check if your GPU can run any model (LLMs, Diffusion, Audio) before downloading cuDNN Detection Finds cuDNN libraries, validates symlinks, checks version compatibility Migration Helper Scans code for deprecated imports (LangChain, Pydantic) and suggests fixes

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pip install env-doctor

Or install from source:

git clone https://github.com/mitulgarg/env-doctor.git
cd env-doctor
pip install -e .

docs.title: Quick start (Other commands explained extensively seperately)
docs.text:
# Diagnose your environment
env-doctor check

# Get safe install command for PyTorch
env-doctor install torch

# Check if a model fits on your GPU
env-doctor model llama-3-8b

docs.title: Quick Sneak Peek (There's WAY more to Env-Doctor)

docs.title: Adding Models to env-doctor
docs.text: This guide explains how to add new AI models to env-doctor's model compatibility database.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Models are stored in src/env_doctor/data/model_requirements.json. To add a model, you only need its parameter count — everything else is optional!

docs.title: Minimum Required Data
docs.text:
{
  "your-model-name": {
    "params_b": 7.0,
    "category": "llm",
    "family": "your-model-family"
  }
}
  • model-name (key): Use lowercase with hyphens (e.g., llama-3-8b)
  • params_b: Model size in billions of parameters (e.g., 7.0 for 7 billion)
  • category: One of: llm, diffusion, audio, language
  • family: Model family (e.g., llama-3, mistral, stable-diffusion)

docs.title: Full Entry Example
docs.text:
For maximum accuracy, include measured VRAM values:

{
  "llama-3-8b": {
    "params_b": 8.0,
    "category": "llm",
    "family": "llama-3",
    "hf_id": "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B",
    "vram": {
      "fp16": 19200,
      "int4": 4800
    },
    "notes": "Instruction-tuned variant, best for instruction following"
  }
}

Optional fields: - hf_id: HuggingFace model ID (for reference links) - vram: Measured VRAM in MB for specific precisions (see below) - notes: Implementation details or usage notes

docs.title: Option 1: HuggingFace Model Card (Recommended)
docs.text:
1. Visit the model on HuggingFace: https://huggingface.co/[author]/[model-name]
  2. Look for the parameter count in:
  3. Model description/tags (often shows "70B" or "Parameters: 70 billion")
  4. Model card text
  5. Technical specs section

Example search: 

https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-70B

docs.title: Option 2: Google Search
docs.text:
"[model name] parameters billion" OR "[model name] size"

Example: "Llama 3 70B parameters" → Result: "70 billion"

docs.title: Option 3: GitHub Repository
docs.text: Check the model's official GitHub repository: - Look in README.md - Check model_config.json files - Search for "params" or "parameters"

docs.title: Option 4: Official Documentation
docs.text: Most model providers publish parameter counts in: - Official model cards - Research papers (usually in abstract or table) - Blog posts announcing the model

docs.title: Adding Measured VRAM Values (Optional, Advanced)
docs.text: If you have access to the model and a GPU, you can measure actual VRAM usage for more accuracy.

docs.title: How to Measure VRAM
docs.text:
1. Load model in specific precision:
import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

model_name = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B"
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_name,
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,  # or torch.bfloat16, torch.float32
    device_map="auto"
)
  1. Check VRAM usage:
nvidia-smi

Look for the GPU memory usage of your process.

  1. Record the value:
{
  "vram": {
    "fp16": 19200,  # in MB
    "int4": 4800
  }
}

docs.title: VRAM Values Format
docs.text:
• Key: Precision (fp32, fp16, bf16, int8, int4, fp8)
  • Value: VRAM in MB (megabytes), not GB

Example conversions: - 19.2 GB = 19,200 MB - 4.8 GB = 4,800 MB - 140 GB = 140,000 MB

docs.title: Which Precisions to Measure?
docs.text: Start with the most common: 1. fp16 - Standard inference precision 2. int4 - Quantized, most popular for memory efficiency 3. int8 - 8-bit quantized 4. bf16 - Brain float (if model supports it)

docs.title: Model Categories
docs.text:
Choose the most appropriate category:

Category Examples Use When llm Llama, Mistral, Qwen, Mixtral Language model for text generation diffusion Stable Diffusion, FLUX, Kandinsky Image generation or image manipulation audio Whisper, Musicgen Speech recognition, generation, or processing language BERT, T5, RoBERTa Text encoding, classification, or small language tasks

docs.title: Model Naming Conventions
docs.text:
Do: - Use lowercase: llama-3-8b ✅ - Use hyphens: stable-diffusion-xl ✅ - Include size: mixtral-8x7b ✅ - Be descriptive: bert-base-uncased ✅

Don't: - Use spaces: llama 3 8b ❌ - Use underscores for separation: llama_3_8b ❌ - Abbreviate: sd-xl instead of stable-diffusion-xl ❌ - Include version number: v1, v2.0 ❌ (unless it's part of official name)

docs.title: Adding Aliases
docs.text:
Help users find models with alternative names:

{
  "aliases": {
    "llama3-8b": "llama-3-8b",
    "sdxl": "stable-diffusion-xl",
    "mistral-7b-v01": "mistral-7b"
  }
}

Aliases are case-insensitive and automatically resolved.

docs.title: Database Schema Validation
docs.text:
All submitted models must pass validation:

# Run validation tests
pytest tests/unit/test_vram_calculator.py::TestVRAMCalculatorDatabaseIntegrity -v

Automatic checks: - ✅ All models have params_b > 0 - ✅ All category values are valid - ✅ All aliases point to existing models - ✅ VRAM values are reasonable (0 < x < 1,000,000 MB) - ✅ Parameter counts are within realistic ranges

docs.title: Step 1: Create Your Branch
docs.text: git checkout -b feature/add-models

docs.title: Step 2: Edit the Database
docs.text:
Add your models to src/env_doctor/data/model_requirements.json:

nano src/env_doctor/data/model_requirements.json
# or use your preferred editor

docs.title: Step 3: Test Your Changes
docs.text:
# Run database validation tests
pytest tests/unit/test_vram_calculator.py::TestVRAMCalculatorDatabaseIntegrity -v

# Test the model works via CLI
env-doctor model your-model-name
env-doctor model --list | grep your-model-name

docs.title: Step 4: Commit and Push
docs.text:
git add src/env_doctor/data/model_requirements.json
git commit -m "feat: add [model-name] and variants to model database"
git push origin feature/add-models

docs.title: Step 5: Create a Pull Request
docs.text:
Include in your PR description: - Which models were added - Parameter counts and sources - Any measured VRAM values included - Models tested (if you have GPU access)

Example PR title: 

feat: add Llama-3 variants (8B, 70B, 405B) to model database

docs.title: Q: What if I don't know exact parameter count?
docs.text: A: Use a reasonable estimate or measurement. For example: - Look at model size comparisons to known models - Check papers or documentation - Estimate from download size (rough: GB file size × 2 ≈ parameters)

docs.title: Q: Can I add multiple models in one PR?
docs.text: A: Yes! Grouping related models (e.g., all Llama-3 variants) is encouraged.

docs.title: Q: How do I find VRAM requirements if I don't have a GPU?
docs.text: A: The formula-based calculation is good enough for initial release. Many users measure and contribute VRAM values later.

docs.title: Q: What if a model has multiple variants (quantized, instruct, chat)?
docs.text:
A: Add the base model. If variants have significantly different parameter counts, add them separately:

{
  "llama-3-8b": { "params_b": 8.0, ... },
  "llama-3-8b-instruct": { "params_b": 8.0, ... }
}

docs.title: Q: How do I find the HuggingFace model ID?
docs.text:
A: It's in the URL on HuggingFace:

https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B
                     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^  <- This is the hf_id

docs.title: Q: What's the correct value for "family"?
docs.text: A: Use the model series name, lowercase with hyphens: - llama-3 (not llama3 or llama 3) - stable-diffusion (not sd or stable-diff) - mistral (single models don't need numbers)

docs.title: Adding a Single Model
docs.text:
{
  "qwen-7b": {
    "params_b": 7.0,
    "category": "llm",
    "family": "qwen",
    "hf_id": "Qwen/Qwen-7B"
  }
}

docs.title: Adding a Model Family with Variants
docs.text:
{
  "mixtral-8x7b": {
    "params_b": 46.7,
    "category": "llm",
    "family": "mixtral",
    "hf_id": "mistralai/Mixtral-8x7B-v0.1",
    "notes": "Mixture of Experts: 46.7B total, 12.9B active"
  },
  "mixtral-8x22b": {
    "params_b": 176.0,
    "category": "llm",
    "family": "mixtral",
    "hf_id": "mistral-community/Mixtral-8x22B-v0.1",
    "vram": {
      "fp16": 263000
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Adding with Measured VRAM
docs.text:
{
  "stable-diffusion-xl": {
    "params_b": 3.5,
    "category": "diffusion",
    "family": "stable-diffusion",
    "hf_id": "stabilityai/stable-diffusion-xl-base-1.0",
    "vram": {
      "fp16": 8000
    },
    "notes": "SDXL base model, improved quality over v1.5"
  }
}

docs.title: Need Help?
docs.text:
• 📚 Check existing models in src/env_doctor/data/model_requirements.json
  • 🐛 Open an issue if you find errors
  • 💬 Discuss in GitHub discussions for questions
  • 🔗 Reference HuggingFace model cards for official specs

docs.title: Contribution Recognition
docs.text:
Thank you for contributing! Your additions help the community: - Make env-doctor more useful for more models - Build an accurate VRAM database - Enable better recommendations for all users

Contributors are recognized in: - GitHub commit history - Pull request discussions - Model database comments (if notable measurements included)

docs.title: Future Enhancements
docs.text: Models you add today enable future features like: - Fine-tuning VRAM requirements - Batch size optimization recommendations - Context length impact on VRAM - Multi-GPU sharding strategies

docs.title: Container Best Practices for GPU Workloads
docs.text: This guide explains best practices for running GPU workloads in Docker containers, and why the env-doctor validators flag certain patterns.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
• Base Images
  • PyTorch Installation
  • TensorFlow Installation
  • Driver Management
  • CUDA Toolkit
  • Docker Compose Configuration
  • Host System Requirements

docs.title: ✅ Use GPU-Enabled Base Images
docs.text:
Always use a CUDA-enabled base image for GPU workloads.

# ✅ GOOD - NVIDIA CUDA base image
FROM nvidia/cuda:12.1.0-runtime-ubuntu22.04

# ✅ GOOD - PyTorch official image
FROM pytorch/pytorch:2.1.0-cuda12.1-cudnn8-runtime

# ✅ GOOD - TensorFlow GPU image
FROM tensorflow/tensorflow:latest-gpu

Why? These images include the CUDA runtime libraries needed to communicate with the GPU driver.

docs.title: ❌ Don't Use CPU-Only Images
docs.text:
# ❌ BAD - CPU-only image
FROM python:3.10

# ❌ BAD - Generic Ubuntu
FROM ubuntu:22.04

# ❌ BAD - Alpine (no CUDA support)
FROM alpine:latest

Why? These images lack CUDA runtime libraries. Your GPU code will fail at runtime with errors like: - CUDA not available - No CUDA-capable device detected - Could not load dynamic library 'libcudart.so'

docs.title: Runtime vs Devel Images
docs.text:
Choose the right image variant:

  • Runtime (-runtime): For running pre-trained models. Smaller size (~2-3GB).
  • Devel (-devel): For compiling CUDA code (flash-attention, xformers). Larger size (~6-8GB).
# For inference only
FROM nvidia/cuda:12.1.0-runtime-ubuntu22.04

# For training with custom CUDA extensions
FROM nvidia/cuda:12.1.0-devel-ubuntu22.04

docs.title: ✅ Always Use --index-url
docs.text:
Never install PyTorch without specifying the CUDA version.

# ✅ GOOD - Explicit CUDA version
RUN pip install torch torchvision --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu121

# ✅ GOOD - CUDA 11.8
RUN pip install torch torchvision --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu118

docs.title: ❌ Don't Install Without --index-url
docs.text:
# ❌ BAD - May download wrong CUDA version
RUN pip install torch torchvision

# ❌ BAD - Defaults to latest, may mismatch driver
RUN pip install torch

Why? Without --index-url, pip defaults to the latest CUDA version (often 12.4+), which may not match: - Your base image's CUDA version - Your host driver's supported CUDA version

This causes runtime errors like: - CUDA driver version is insufficient - no kernel image is available for execution

docs.title: Match Base Image CUDA Version
docs.text:
If your base image is nvidia/cuda:11.8, use cu118:

FROM nvidia/cuda:11.8.0-runtime-ubuntu22.04
RUN pip install torch --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu118

docs.title: CUDA Version Mapping
docs.text: Base Image CUDA --index-url suffix 11.7.x cu117 11.8.x cu118 12.0.x cu120 12.1.x cu121 12.2.x cu122

docs.title: ✅ Use tensorflow[and-cuda]
docs.text:
# ✅ GOOD - Explicit GPU support
RUN pip install tensorflow[and-cuda]

# ✅ ALSO GOOD - Plain tensorflow with CUDA available
RUN pip install tensorflow

docs.title: ⚠️ Avoid tensorflow-gpu
docs.text:
# ⚠️ DEPRECATED - Old package name
RUN pip install tensorflow-gpu

Why? tensorflow-gpu is deprecated. Modern TensorFlow (2.0+) automatically uses GPU when available. Use tensorflow[and-cuda] to ensure CUDA dependencies are installed.

docs.title: ❌ NEVER Install NVIDIA Drivers in Containers
docs.text:
# ❌ WRONG - Don't install drivers
RUN apt-get install -y nvidia-driver-535

# ❌ WRONG - Don't install any nvidia-driver-*
RUN apt-get install -y nvidia-driver-*

Why? 1. Drivers must be on the host, not in containers 2. Container shares host's kernel and GPU driver 3. Installing drivers in container: - Wastes space (~500MB) - Can cause conflicts - Won't work (container can't load kernel modules)

docs.title: ✅ Correct Driver Setup
docs.text:
• Host: Install NVIDIA driver on host machine
  • Container: Use CUDA-enabled base image (has runtime libraries)
  • Docker: Install nvidia-container-toolkit on host
# On host machine (Ubuntu/Debian)
sudo apt-get install -y nvidia-driver-535
sudo apt-get install -y nvidia-container-toolkit
sudo systemctl restart docker

docs.title: ⚠️ Toolkit Usually Not Needed
docs.text:
Most containers don't need the full CUDA Toolkit.

# ⚠️ USUALLY UNNECESSARY - Adds 2-5GB
RUN apt-get install -y cuda-toolkit-12-1

Why? - Runtime-only containers have everything needed to run GPU code - Toolkit is only for compiling CUDA code - Adds significant image bloat

docs.title: ✅ Only Install If Compiling
docs.text:
Install toolkit only when building from source:

# ✅ GOOD - Needed for flash-attention
FROM nvidia/cuda:12.1.0-devel-ubuntu22.04

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    cuda-toolkit-12-1 \
    build-essential

RUN pip install flash-attn --no-build-isolation

When you need toolkit: - Building flash-attention - Building xformers - Compiling custom CUDA kernels - Building auto-gptq from source

When you DON'T need it: - Installing PyTorch/TensorFlow wheels (pre-compiled) - Running inference - Running training with standard ops

docs.title: ✅ Modern GPU Configuration (Compose v2.3+)
docs.text:
version: '3.8'

services:
  gpu-app:
    image: nvidia/cuda:12.1.0-runtime-ubuntu22.04
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: all  # or 1, 2, etc.
              capabilities: [gpu]
    command: python train.py

Required fields: - driver: nvidia - Specifies NVIDIA GPU - count: all or specific number - How many GPUs - capabilities: [gpu] - Enable GPU capability

docs.title: ❌ Deprecated Syntax
docs.text:
# ❌ OLD - Deprecated in Compose v2.3+
services:
  old-app:
    image: nvidia/cuda:12.1.0-runtime-ubuntu22.04
    runtime: nvidia

Why deprecated? - Less flexible (can't control GPU allocation) - Removed in Compose v3+ - Doesn't support advanced features (device selection, memory limits)

docs.title: Multi-GPU Services
docs.text:
Assign specific GPUs to different services:

version: '3.8'

services:
  training:
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              device_ids: ['0', '1']  # First two GPUs
              capabilities: [gpu]

  inference:
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              device_ids: ['2']  # Third GPU
              capabilities: [gpu]

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before running GPU containers, ensure your host has:

  1. NVIDIA Driver 

    nvidia-smi  # Should show driver version and GPUs

  2. nvidia-container-toolkit 

    # Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt-get install -y nvidia-container-toolkit

# RHEL/CentOS
sudo yum install -y nvidia-container-toolkit

  3. Docker Daemon Configuration

Create/edit /etc/docker/daemon.json: 

{
  "runtimes": {
    "nvidia": {
      "path": "nvidia-container-runtime",
      "runtimeArgs": []
    }
  }
}

Restart Docker: 

sudo systemctl restart docker

docs.title: Verify Setup
docs.text:
Test GPU access in a container:

docker run --rm --gpus all nvidia/cuda:12.1.0-runtime-ubuntu22.04 nvidia-smi

Should display GPU information.

docs.title: Issue: "CUDA not available" in container
docs.text:
Causes: - Using CPU-only base image - nvidia-container-toolkit not installed - Missing --gpus all flag (when running docker run) - Missing GPU device config (in docker-compose)

Solutions: 1. Use CUDA-enabled base image 2. Install nvidia-container-toolkit on host 3. Add GPU configuration to docker-compose.yml

docs.title: Issue: "CUDA driver version insufficient"
docs.text:
Causes: - PyTorch/TensorFlow built for newer CUDA than driver supports - Missing --index-url in pip install

Solution: 

# Check driver's max CUDA version
nvidia-smi | grep "CUDA Version"

# Install matching PyTorch
# If driver supports CUDA 11.8:
pip install torch --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu118

docs.title: Issue: Image too large
docs.text:
Causes: - Using -devel images when -runtime is sufficient - Installing CUDA toolkit unnecessarily

Solutions: 1. Use -runtime images for inference 2. Only install toolkit when compiling CUDA code 3. Use multi-stage builds to copy only needed artifacts

docs.title: Quick Validation
docs.text:
Before building your container, run:

# Validate Dockerfile
env-doctor dockerfile

# Validate docker-compose.yml
env-doctor docker-compose

Fix all errors before building to avoid runtime issues.

docs.title: Additional Resources
docs.text:
• NVIDIA Container Toolkit Documentation
  • Docker Compose GPU Access
  • PyTorch Docker Images
  • TensorFlow Docker Images

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: Understanding how Env-Doctor works internally.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
flowchart TB
    CLI["CLI<br/>(cli.py)"]
    REG["Detector Registry<br/>(Discovers & runs detectors)"]

    CLI --> REG

    REG --> WSL["WSL2<br/>Detector"]
    REG --> DRV["Driver<br/>Detector"]
    REG --> CUDA["CUDA<br/>Detector"]
    REG --> PY["Python<br/>Detector"]

    REG --> DB["Compatibility Database<br/>(compatibility.json)"]

docs.title: The CLI (cli.py)
docs.text: Entry point for all commands. Orchestrates detectors and presents unified diagnostics.

docs.title: The Detector Registry
docs.text:
Plugin system for detector discovery and execution. Allows easy addition of new detectors.

from env_doctor.registry import DetectorRegistry

registry = DetectorRegistry()
results = registry.run_all()

docs.title: Detectors
docs.text:
Modular detection system with specialized detectors:

Detector Purpose WSL2Detector Environment detection, GPU forwarding validation NvidiaDriverDetector GPU driver version and capability detection CudaToolkitDetector System CUDA installation detection CudnnDetector cuDNN library detection and validation PythonLibraryDetector Python AI library version and CUDA compatibility

Each detector returns a DetectorResult with:

  • status: SUCCESS, WARNING, ERROR, NOT_FOUND
  • component: What was detected
  • version: Version string
  • path: Installation path (if applicable)
  • metadata: Additional detection details

docs.title: The Compatibility Database (compatibility.json)
docs.text:
Maps drivers to maximum supported CUDA versions and verified wheel URLs:

{
  "cuda_driver_mapping": {
    "535.104.05": "12.2",
    "525.85.12": "12.0",
    "470.82.01": "11.4"
  },
  "pytorch_wheels": {
    "cu121": "https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu121",
    "cu118": "https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu118"
  }
}

docs.title: The Updater (db.py)
docs.text: Fetches the latest compatibility rules from GitHub so you don't need to update the package frequently.

docs.title: Automated Database Updates
docs.text: The compatibility database is maintained through automation:

docs.title: Scraper (tools/scraper.py)
docs.text:
• GitHub Actions runs periodically
  • Scrapes official PyTorch/TensorFlow/JAX documentation
  • Extracts CUDA compatibility mappings
  • Creates pull requests with updates

docs.title: Validator (tools/validator.py) (Not Implemented yet)
docs.text:
• Validates scraped data structure
  • Ensures version strings are parseable
  • Catches malformed entries

docs.title: Human Review
docs.text:
• Automated updates create PRs (not auto-merged)
  • Maintainers review before merging
  • Community can flag issues

docs.title: Adding a New Detector
docs.text:
1. Create a detector class:
from env_doctor.detectors.base import BaseDetector, DetectorResult, Status

class MyDetector(BaseDetector):
    name = "my_component"

    def detect(self) -> DetectorResult:
        # Detection logic here
        return DetectorResult(
            status=Status.SUCCESS,
            component=self.name,
            version="1.0.0",
            metadata={"key": "value"}
        )

  1. Register in the registry (automatic via naming convention)

  2. Add to CLI if needed

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Contributing Guide
  • debug Command - View raw detector output

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This guide will help you install Env-Doctor and run your first environment check.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.7 or higher
  • NVIDIA GPU (optional, but recommended)
  • NVIDIA driver installed (for GPU features)

docs.title: From PyPI (Recommended)
docs.text: pip install env-doctor

docs.title: From Source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/mitulgarg/env-doctor.git
cd env-doctor
pip install -e .

docs.title: Your First Check
docs.text:
Run the environment check to see your current GPU/CUDA status:

env-doctor check

This command checks:

  • Environment: Native Linux vs WSL1 vs WSL2, with GPU forwarding validation
  • GPU Driver: Version and maximum supported CUDA version
  • System CUDA: Installation status and version
  • Library Conflicts: Detects mismatched versions (e.g., PyTorch built for CUDA 12.4 on a driver supporting only CUDA 11.8)
  • WSL2 GPU Setup: Validates CUDA libraries and driver configuration

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
🩺 ENV-DOCTOR DIAGNOSIS
============================================================

🖥️  Environment: Native Linux

🎮 GPU Driver
   ✅ NVIDIA Driver: 535.146.02
   └─ Max CUDA: 12.2

🔧 CUDA Toolkit
   ✅ System CUDA: 12.1.1
   └─ Path: /usr/local/cuda-12.1

📦 Python Libraries
   ✅ torch 2.1.0+cu121
   ✅ tensorflow 2.15.0

✅ All checks passed! Your environment is healthy.

docs.title: Get a Safe Install Command
docs.text:
If you need to install or reinstall PyTorch:

env-doctor install torch

This outputs the exact pip install command with the correct --index-url for your driver.

docs.title: Check Model Compatibility
docs.text:
Before downloading a large model:

env-doctor model llama-3-8b

docs.title: Validate Your Dockerfile
docs.text:
Before building a GPU container:

env-doctor dockerfile path/to/Dockerfile

docs.title: Quick Command Reference
docs.text: Command Purpose env-doctor check Full environment diagnosis env-doctor install <lib> Get safe install command env-doctor model <name> Check model VRAM requirements env-doctor cuda-info Detailed CUDA toolkit analysis env-doctor cudnn-info cuDNN library analysis env-doctor dockerfile Validate Dockerfile env-doctor docker-compose Validate docker-compose.yml env-doctor scan Scan for deprecated imports env-doctor debug Verbose detector output

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Command Reference - Detailed documentation for each command
  • WSL2 Guide - GPU setup for Windows Subsystem for Linux
  • CI/CD Integration - Use Env-Doctor in your pipelines

docs.title: check
docs.text: Diagnose your environment for GPU/CUDA compatibility issues.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: env-doctor check

docs.title: Environment Detection
docs.text:
• Native Linux: Standard Linux environment
  • WSL1: Detects WSL1 and warns that CUDA is not supported
  • WSL2: Full GPU forwarding validation

docs.title: GPU Driver
docs.text:
• Driver version detection via NVML
  • Maximum supported CUDA version
  • Driver health status

docs.title: System CUDA Toolkit
docs.text:
• Installation path and version
  • Multiple installation detection
  • PATH and environment configuration

docs.title: Python Libraries
docs.text:
• PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX detection
  • CUDA version each library was compiled for
  • Compatibility with your driver

docs.title: Library Conflicts
docs.text:
Detects "Frankenstein" environments where:

  • PyTorch is built for CUDA 12.4 but driver only supports 11.8
  • Multiple libraries compiled for different CUDA versions
  • System toolkit doesn't match library requirements

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
🩺 ENV-DOCTOR DIAGNOSIS
============================================================

🖥️  Environment: WSL2 (GPU forwarding enabled)

🎮 GPU Driver
   ✅ NVIDIA Driver: 535.146.02
   └─ Max CUDA: 12.2

🔧 CUDA Toolkit
   ✅ System CUDA: 12.1.1
   └─ Path: /usr/local/cuda-12.1

📦 Python Libraries
   ✅ torch 2.1.0+cu121
   └─ CUDA 12.1 ✓ (compatible with driver)

✅ All checks passed!

docs.title: Driver Too Old
docs.text:
❌ PyTorch requires CUDA 12.1 but driver only supports CUDA 11.8
   → Update your NVIDIA driver to 520.61.05 or newer
   → Or install PyTorch for CUDA 11.8:
     pip install torch --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu118

docs.title: Missing CUDA Toolkit
docs.text:
⚠️  No system CUDA toolkit found
   → This is OK if you only use PyTorch/TensorFlow (they bundle CUDA)
   → Install CUDA toolkit if you need to compile extensions

docs.title: WSL2 GPU Issues
docs.text:
❌ NVIDIA driver installed inside WSL. This breaks GPU forwarding.
   → Run: sudo apt remove --purge nvidia-*

docs.title: JSON Output
docs.text:
For scripting or parsing results programmatically:

env-doctor check --json

{
  "status": "success",
  "timestamp": "2026-01-15T10:30:00Z",
  "summary": {
    "driver": "found",
    "cuda": "found",
    "issues_count": 0
  },
  "checks": {
    "driver": {
      "component": "nvidia_driver",
      "status": "success",
      "detected": true,
      "version": "535.146.02",
      "metadata": {
        "max_cuda_version": "12.2"
      }
    },
    "cuda": {
      "component": "cuda_toolkit",
      "status": "success",
      "detected": true,
      "version": "12.1.1",
      "path": "/usr/local/cuda-12.1"
    },
    "libraries": {
      "torch": {
        "version": "2.1.0+cu121",
        "cuda_version": "12.1",
        "compatible": true
      }
    }
  }
}

docs.title: CI/CD Mode
docs.text:
For continuous integration pipelines:

env-doctor check --ci

This implies --json and sets proper exit codes:

Code Meaning 0 All checks passed 1 Warnings or non-critical issues 2 Critical errors detected

See CI/CD Integration Guide for full pipeline examples.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• cuda-info - Detailed CUDA toolkit analysis
  • cudnn-info - cuDNN library analysis
  • debug - Verbose detector output

docs.title: cuda-info
docs.text: Get detailed information about your CUDA toolkit installations.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: env-doctor cuda-info

docs.title: CUDA Installations
docs.text:
• All CUDA toolkit installations found on your system
  • Version of each installation
  • Installation paths

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
• CUDA_HOME configuration
  • PATH entries for CUDA binaries
  • LD_LIBRARY_PATH (Linux) or PATH (Windows) for libraries

docs.title: Runtime Libraries
docs.text:
• libcudart (CUDA runtime) status
  • Library paths and versions

docs.title: Compatibility
docs.text:
• Driver compatibility with installed toolkit(s)
  • Potential version conflicts

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
🔧 CUDA TOOLKIT ANALYSIS
============================================================

📍 Installations Found:

  CUDA 12.1.1
  └─ Path: /usr/local/cuda-12.1
  └─ nvcc: /usr/local/cuda-12.1/bin/nvcc

  CUDA 11.8.0
  └─ Path: /usr/local/cuda-11.8
  └─ nvcc: /usr/local/cuda-11.8/bin/nvcc

🌐 Environment Configuration:

  CUDA_HOME: /usr/local/cuda-12.1
  PATH:
    ✅ /usr/local/cuda-12.1/bin
  LD_LIBRARY_PATH:
    ✅ /usr/local/cuda-12.1/lib64

📚 Runtime Libraries:

  libcudart.so.12.1.105
  └─ Path: /usr/local/cuda-12.1/lib64/libcudart.so.12

🎮 Driver Compatibility:

  Driver: 535.146.02 (supports up to CUDA 12.2)
  ✅ CUDA 12.1 is compatible with your driver

docs.title: Multiple Installations
docs.text:
When multiple CUDA versions are installed:

⚠️  Multiple CUDA installations detected

  Active: CUDA 12.1 (via CUDA_HOME)
  Also found: CUDA 11.8

💡 Recommendations:
  - Ensure CUDA_HOME points to the version you need
  - Check PATH order if using nvcc

docs.title: CUDA_HOME Not Set
docs.text:
❌ CUDA_HOME is not set

💡 Fix:
  export CUDA_HOME=/usr/local/cuda-12.1
  export PATH=$CUDA_HOME/bin:$PATH

docs.title: PATH Mismatch
docs.text:
⚠️  PATH contains different CUDA version than CUDA_HOME

  CUDA_HOME: /usr/local/cuda-12.1
  PATH nvcc: /usr/local/cuda-11.8/bin/nvcc

💡 Fix:
  Ensure PATH includes $CUDA_HOME/bin before other CUDA paths

docs.title: JSON Output
docs.text: env-doctor cuda-info --json

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• cudnn-info - cuDNN library analysis
  • check - Full environment diagnosis
  • debug - Verbose detector output

docs.title: cudnn-info
docs.text: Detect and validate cuDNN library installations.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: env-doctor cudnn-info

docs.title: cuDNN Detection
docs.text:
• cuDNN version and build information
  • Library file locations
  • Header file locations

docs.title: Installation Validation
docs.text:
• Multiple installation detection
  • Symlink validation (Linux)
  • PATH configuration (Windows)

docs.title: Compatibility
docs.text:
• CUDA version compatibility
  • Version requirements for popular frameworks

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
🧠 cuDNN ANALYSIS
============================================================

📚 cuDNN Installation:

  Version: 8.9.7
  CUDA Version: 12.x

  Libraries:
    ✅ libcudnn.so.8.9.7
    └─ Path: /usr/local/cuda-12.1/lib64/libcudnn.so.8

  Headers:
    ✅ cudnn.h
    └─ Path: /usr/local/cuda-12.1/include/cudnn.h

🔗 Symlinks (Linux):

  libcudnn.so → libcudnn.so.8 → libcudnn.so.8.9.7
  ✅ Symlink chain is valid

🎮 Compatibility:

  ✅ cuDNN 8.9.7 is compatible with CUDA 12.1
  ✅ Meets PyTorch 2.1+ requirements (cuDNN 8.5+)
  ✅ Meets TensorFlow 2.15+ requirements (cuDNN 8.6+)

docs.title: Multiple Installations
docs.text:
⚠️  Multiple cuDNN installations detected

  /usr/local/cuda-12.1/lib64/libcudnn.so.8.9.7
  /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcudnn.so.8.6.0

💡 Recommendations:
  - Ensure LD_LIBRARY_PATH prioritizes the correct version
  - Consider removing older installations to avoid conflicts

docs.title: Missing cuDNN
docs.text:
❌ cuDNN not found

💡 Installation:
  1. Download from https://developer.nvidia.com/cudnn
  2. Extract to /usr/local/cuda-12.1/
  3. Or install via package manager:
     sudo apt install libcudnn8 libcudnn8-dev

docs.title: Broken Symlinks
docs.text:
❌ Broken symlink detected

  libcudnn.so → libcudnn.so.8 (missing)

💡 Fix:
  cd /usr/local/cuda/lib64
  sudo ln -sf libcudnn.so.8.9.7 libcudnn.so.8
  sudo ln -sf libcudnn.so.8 libcudnn.so

docs.title: Version Mismatch
docs.text:
⚠️  cuDNN version may not be optimal

  Installed: cuDNN 8.2.0
  PyTorch 2.1 recommends: cuDNN 8.5+

💡 Consider upgrading cuDNN for better performance

docs.title: JSON Output
docs.text: env-doctor cudnn-info --json

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• cuda-info - CUDA toolkit analysis
  • check - Full environment diagnosis

docs.title: debug
docs.text: Get detailed information from all detectors for troubleshooting.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: env-doctor debug

docs.title: All Detector Results
docs.text:
• Raw output from every registered detector
  • Detection status (success, warning, error, not_found)
  • Version information

docs.title: Detection Metadata
docs.text:
• Internal detection methods used
  • File paths examined
  • Detailed status information

docs.title: Registry Information
docs.text:
• List of all available detectors
  • Detector execution order

docs.title: Error Details
docs.text:
• Full exception traces
  • Diagnostic information for failures

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
🔍 DEBUG MODE - Detailed Detector Information
============================================================
Registered Detectors: cuda_toolkit, nvidia_driver, python_library, wsl2, cudnn

--- WSL2 ---
Status: Status.SUCCESS
Component: wsl2
Version: wsl2
Metadata: {
  'environment': 'WSL2',
  'gpu_forwarding': 'enabled',
  'wsl_lib_path': '/usr/lib/wsl/lib',
  'libcuda_found': True
}

--- NVIDIA DRIVER ---
Status: Status.SUCCESS
Component: nvidia_driver
Version: 535.146.02
Path: /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libnvidia-ml.so.1
Metadata: {
  'max_cuda_version': '12.2',
  'detection_method': 'nvml',
  'gpu_name': 'NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090',
  'gpu_memory': 24576
}

--- CUDA TOOLKIT ---
Status: Status.SUCCESS
Component: cuda_toolkit
Version: 12.1.1
Path: /usr/local/cuda-12.1
Metadata: {
  'nvcc_path': '/usr/local/cuda-12.1/bin/nvcc',
  'cuda_home': '/usr/local/cuda-12.1',
  'multiple_installations': ['/usr/local/cuda-12.1', '/usr/local/cuda-11.8']
}

--- CUDNN ---
Status: Status.SUCCESS
Component: cudnn
Version: 8.9.7
Path: /usr/local/cuda-12.1/lib64/libcudnn.so.8
Metadata: {
  'cuda_version': '12.x',
  'header_path': '/usr/local/cuda-12.1/include/cudnn.h'
}

--- PYTHON LIBRARIES ---
Status: Status.SUCCESS
Component: python_library
Libraries: {
  'torch': {
    'version': '2.1.0+cu121',
    'cuda_version': '12.1',
    'detection_method': 'import'
  },
  'tensorflow': {
    'status': 'not_installed'
  }
}

docs.title: Unexpected Results
docs.text:
When env-doctor check shows results that don't match your expectations:

# Regular check shows CUDA not found, but you know it's installed
env-doctor check
# Shows: ⚠️ No system CUDA toolkit found

# Debug to see what happened
env-doctor debug
# Shows: Detection attempted at /usr/local/cuda (symlink broken)

docs.title: Contributing or Reporting Issues
docs.text:
Include debug output when:

  • Reporting bugs
  • Contributing new detectors
  • Validating behavior changes

docs.title: Understanding Detection Methods
docs.text:
Debug shows how each component was detected:

  • NVML for driver detection
  • File system search for CUDA
  • Python imports for libraries

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• check - Standard environment check
  • Architecture - How detectors work

docs.title: docker-compose
docs.text: Validate docker-compose.yml files for proper GPU device configuration.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
env-doctor docker-compose [PATH]

If no path is provided, looks for docker-compose.yml or docker-compose.yaml in the current directory.

docs.title: GPU Device Configuration
docs.text:
• Checks for deploy.resources.reservations.devices
  • Validates driver: nvidia is specified
  • Ensures capabilities: [gpu] is set

docs.title: Deprecated Syntax
docs.text:
• Flags old runtime: nvidia approach
  • Provides migration path to new syntax

docs.title: Multi-Service Configuration
docs.text:
• Warns about GPU resource sharing between services
  • Validates each service with GPU requirements

docs.title: Host Requirements
docs.text:
• Checks for nvidia-container-toolkit
  • Validates Docker GPU support

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
🐳  DOCKER COMPOSE VALIDATION: docker-compose.yml

❌  ERRORS (1):
------------------------------------------------------------

Service 'ml-training':
  Issue: Missing GPU device configuration
  Fix:   Add GPU device configuration under deploy.resources.reservations.devices

  Suggested fix:
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: all
              capabilities: [gpu]

⚠️   WARNINGS (1):
------------------------------------------------------------

Service 'legacy-app':
  Issue: Deprecated 'runtime: nvidia' syntax
  Fix:   Use the new 'deploy.resources.reservations.devices' syntax instead

SUMMARY:
  ❌ Errors:   1
  ⚠️  Warnings: 1
  ℹ️  Info:     0

docs.title: Modern Syntax (Compose v2.4+)
docs.text:
services:
  ml-training:
    image: pytorch/pytorch:2.1.0-cuda12.1-cudnn8-runtime
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: all  # or specific number: count: 1
              capabilities: [gpu]

docs.title: Device Selection
docs.text:
# Use all GPUs
count: all

# Use specific number of GPUs
count: 2

# Use specific GPU by ID
device_ids: ['0', '2']

docs.title: Deprecated Syntax
docs.text:
# ❌ Old syntax (still works but deprecated)
services:
  app:
    runtime: nvidia

# ✅ New syntax
services:
  app:
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              capabilities: [gpu]

docs.title: Missing nvidia-container-toolkit
docs.text:
❌ nvidia-container-toolkit not detected on host

💡 Installation:
  # Ubuntu/Debian
  distribution=$(. /etc/os-release;echo $ID$VERSION_ID)
  curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/nvidia-docker/gpgkey | sudo apt-key add -
  curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/nvidia-docker/$distribution/nvidia-docker.list | \
    sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/nvidia-docker.list
  sudo apt update && sudo apt install nvidia-container-toolkit
  sudo systemctl restart docker

docs.title: GPU Sharing Warning
docs.text:
⚠️  Multiple services reserving GPU resources

  Services: ml-training, inference-api

💡 Note:
  GPU memory is shared between containers.
  Ensure total VRAM requirements don't exceed available memory.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • dockerfile - Validate Dockerfile

docs.title: dockerfile
docs.text: Validate Dockerfiles for GPU/CUDA configuration issues before building.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
env-doctor dockerfile [PATH]

If no path is provided, looks for Dockerfile in the current directory.

docs.title: Base Images
docs.text:
• Detects CPU-only images (python:3.10, ubuntu:22.04)
  • Provides DB-driven GPU base image recommendations
  • Suggests appropriate runtime vs devel images

docs.title: PyTorch Installation
docs.text:
• Ensures pip install torch has correct --index-url
  • Validates version compatibility with base image CUDA
  • Uses verified install commands from the database

docs.title: Library Compatibility
docs.text:
• Validates pinned versions against DB-verified combinations
  • Checks multi-library compatibility (torch + tensorflow + jax)
  • Flags deprecated packages (tensorflow-gpu)

docs.title: Build Requirements
docs.text:
• Detects compilation requirements (flash-attn, xformers)
  • Enforces -devel base images when needed
  • Warns about unnecessary toolkit installs

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Flags NVIDIA driver installs (must be on host)
  • Warns about bloating images with unnecessary packages

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
🐳  DOCKERFILE VALIDATION: Dockerfile

❌  ERRORS (2):
------------------------------------------------------------

Line 1:
  Issue: CPU-only base image detected: python:3.10
  Fix:   Use a GPU-enabled base image

  Suggested fix:
    FROM nvidia/cuda:12.1.0-runtime-ubuntu22.04
    # Or: FROM pytorch/pytorch:2.1.0-cuda12.1-cudnn8-runtime
    # Or: FROM tensorflow/tensorflow:latest-gpu

Line 8:
  Issue: PyTorch installation missing --index-url flag
  Fix:   Add --index-url to install the correct CUDA version

  Suggested fix:
    RUN pip install torch torchvision --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu121

⚠️   WARNINGS (1):
------------------------------------------------------------

Line 15:
  Issue: Installing CUDA toolkit in container
  Fix:   Use a CUDA base image instead to reduce image size

SUMMARY:
  ❌ Errors:   2
  ⚠️  Warnings: 1
  ℹ️  Info:     0

docs.title: Base Image Rules
docs.text: Image Type Recommendation python:* Use nvidia/cuda:* or pytorch/pytorch:* ubuntu:* Use nvidia/cuda:*-base-ubuntu* nvidia/cuda:*-runtime-* Good for inference nvidia/cuda:*-devel-* Required for compilation

docs.title: PyTorch Install Rules
docs.text:
# ❌ Wrong - gets whatever CUDA version pip decides
RUN pip install torch

# ✅ Correct - explicit CUDA version
RUN pip install torch --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu121

docs.title: Compilation Detection
docs.text:
When these packages are detected, -devel base image is required:

  • flash-attn
  • xformers (when building from source)
  • Any package with --no-binary

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • docker-compose - Validate docker-compose.yml

docs.title: install
docs.text: Get the safe install command for GPU libraries that matches your driver.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: env-doctor install <library>

docs.title: Supported Libraries
docs.text:
• torch / pytorch - PyTorch with correct CUDA wheels
  • tensorflow - TensorFlow with GPU support
  • jax - JAX with CUDA support

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Detects your NVIDIA driver version
  2. Determines the maximum CUDA version your driver supports
  3. Looks up the correct wheel URL for that CUDA version
  4. Outputs the exact pip install command

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
env-doctor install torch

Output:

🔍 Detecting your GPU environment...

🎮 GPU Driver: 535.146.02
   └─ Max CUDA: 12.2

⬇️ Run this command to install the SAFE version:
---------------------------------------------------
pip install torch torchvision --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu121
---------------------------------------------------

💡 This installs PyTorch built for CUDA 12.1, which is compatible
   with your driver (supports up to CUDA 12.2).

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
The default pip install torch gives you the latest wheel, which might be built for CUDA 12.4. If your driver only supports CUDA 11.8, you'll get cryptic errors like:

RuntimeError: CUDA error: no kernel image is available for execution on the device

Env-Doctor prevents this by prescribing the correct version for your hardware.

docs.title: Older Driver
docs.text:
$ env-doctor install torch

🎮 GPU Driver: 470.82.01
   └─ Max CUDA: 11.4

⬇️ Run this command to install the SAFE version:
---------------------------------------------------
pip install torch torchvision --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu113
---------------------------------------------------

docs.title: No GPU Detected
docs.text:
$ env-doctor install torch

⚠️  No NVIDIA GPU detected

⬇️ Installing CPU-only version:
---------------------------------------------------
pip install torch torchvision --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu
---------------------------------------------------

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• check - Full environment diagnosis
  • model - Check model VRAM requirements

docs.title: model
docs.text: Check if an AI model fits on your GPU before downloading.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: env-doctor model <model-name>

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Description --list List all available models --precision <type> Check specific precision (fp32, fp16, int8, int4)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
env-doctor model llama-3-8b

Output:

🤖  Checking: LLAMA-3-8B
    Parameters: 8.0B
    HuggingFace: meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B

🖥️   Your Hardware:
    RTX 3090 (24GB VRAM)

💾  VRAM Requirements & Compatibility

  ✅  FP16: 19.2GB (measured) - 4.8GB free
  ✅  INT4:  4.8GB (estimated) - 19.2GB free

✅  This model WILL FIT on your GPU!

💡  Recommendations:
1. Use fp16 for best quality on your GPU

docs.title: Listing Available Models
docs.text:
env-doctor model --list

Output:

📋 Available Models

LLMs:
  llama-3-8b, llama-3-70b, llama-3-405b
  mistral-7b, mixtral-8x7b
  qwen-7b, qwen-14b, qwen-72b

Diffusion:
  stable-diffusion-1.5, stable-diffusion-xl, stable-diffusion-3
  flux-schnell, flux-dev

Audio:
  whisper-tiny, whisper-base, whisper-small
  whisper-medium, whisper-large, whisper-large-v3

Language:
  bert-base, bert-large
  t5-small, t5-base, t5-large

docs.title: Checking Specific Precision
docs.text:
env-doctor model stable-diffusion-xl --precision int4

Output:

🤖  Checking: STABLE-DIFFUSION-XL
    Parameters: 6.6B

🖥️   Your Hardware:
    RTX 3060 (12GB VRAM)

💾  INT4 Requirements:
    ~2.5GB VRAM (estimated)

✅  This model WILL FIT at INT4 precision!

docs.title: LLMs
docs.text:
Large Language Models for text generation:

Model Parameters FP16 VRAM Llama-3-8B 8B ~19GB Llama-3-70B 70B ~140GB Mistral-7B 7B ~16GB Mixtral-8x7B 46.7B ~93GB

docs.title: Diffusion Models
docs.text:
Image generation models:

Model VRAM (FP16) Stable Diffusion 1.5 ~4GB Stable Diffusion XL ~8GB Flux Schnell ~12GB

docs.title: Audio Models
docs.text:
Speech recognition and synthesis:

Model VRAM Whisper Tiny ~1GB Whisper Large ~10GB

docs.title: Model Aliases
docs.text:
Common aliases are supported:

  • sdxl → stable-diffusion-xl
  • sd15 → stable-diffusion-1.5
  • llama3 → llama-3-8b

docs.title: When a Model Won't Fit
docs.text:
env-doctor model llama-3-70b

🤖  Checking: LLAMA-3-70B
    Parameters: 70B

🖥️   Your Hardware:
    RTX 3090 (24GB VRAM)

💾  VRAM Requirements:
    FP16: ~140GB
    INT4: ~35GB

❌  This model will NOT fit on your GPU

💡  Recommendations:
1. Try llama-3-8b (same family, fits in 24GB)
2. Use INT4 quantization with 2x RTX 3090
3. Consider cloud GPU (A100 80GB)

docs.title: How VRAM Is Calculated
docs.text:
For models with measured data, we use real-world VRAM usage from testing.

For other models, we estimate using:

Precision Formula FP32 params × 4 bytes FP16 params × 2 bytes INT8 params × 1 byte + overhead INT4 params × 0.5 bytes + overhead

Overhead

Actual VRAM usage includes KV cache, activations, and framework overhead. Our estimates include a ~20% buffer for this.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• check - Environment diagnosis
  • install - Get safe install commands

docs.title: scan
docs.text: Scan your project for deprecated AI library imports and suggest fixes.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
env-doctor scan [PATH]

If no path is provided, scans the current directory.

docs.title: LangChain Deprecations
docs.text:
• Old import paths from langchain
  • Migration to langchain-core, langchain-community, etc.

docs.title: Pydantic V1 → V2
docs.text:
• Deprecated validator syntax
  • Old import paths

docs.title: Other Library Changes
docs.text:
• TensorFlow 1.x patterns
  • PyTorch deprecated APIs

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
🔍 SCANNING PROJECT: ./my_project

📁 Files scanned: 47
📝 Issues found: 3

------------------------------------------------------------

src/chains/qa.py:12
  ❌ Deprecated import: from langchain.chat_models import ChatOpenAI
  ✅ Replace with: from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

src/chains/qa.py:15
  ❌ Deprecated import: from langchain.embeddings import OpenAIEmbeddings
  ✅ Replace with: from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

src/models/user.py:8
  ⚠️  Pydantic V1 syntax: @validator
  ✅ Replace with: @field_validator (Pydantic V2)

SUMMARY:
  ❌ Errors:   2
  ⚠️  Warnings: 1

docs.title: LangChain
docs.text: Old Import New Import langchain.chat_models langchain_openai or langchain_anthropic langchain.embeddings langchain_openai langchain.vectorstores langchain_community.vectorstores langchain.document_loaders langchain_community.document_loaders

docs.title: Pydantic
docs.text: V1 Syntax V2 Syntax @validator @field_validator @root_validator @model_validator class Config: model_config = ConfigDict(...)

docs.title: Excluding Files
docs.text:
The scan automatically excludes:

  • .git/
  • __pycache__/
  • node_modules/
  • .venv/, venv/
  • *.pyc, *.pyo

docs.title: JSON Output
docs.text: env-doctor scan --json

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: • check - Environment diagnosis

docs.title: CI/CD Integration
docs.text: Env-Doctor supports JSON output and proper exit codes for seamless CI/CD integration.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# GitHub Actions
- run: pip install env-doctor
- run: env-doctor check --ci

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text:
When using --ci flag:

Code Meaning Action 0 All checks passed Continue pipeline 1 Warnings or non-critical issues Review, may continue 2 Critical errors detected Fail pipeline

docs.title: Basic Validation
docs.text:
name: Validate Environment
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.10'
      - run: pip install env-doctor
      - run: env-doctor check --ci

docs.title: GPU Runner Validation
docs.text:
For self-hosted GPU runners:

name: GPU Environment Check
on: [push]

jobs:
  gpu-check:
    runs-on: [self-hosted, gpu]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install env-doctor
      - name: Validate GPU Environment
        run: env-doctor check --ci
      - name: Check Model Compatibility
        run: env-doctor model llama-3-8b --json

docs.title: Dockerfile Validation
docs.text:
name: Validate Docker
on:
  push:
    paths:
      - 'Dockerfile'
      - 'docker-compose.yml'

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install env-doctor
      - name: Validate Dockerfile
        run: env-doctor dockerfile --ci
      - name: Validate Docker Compose
        run: env-doctor docker-compose --ci

docs.title: GitLab CI
docs.text:
stages:
  - validate

validate-environment:
  stage: validate
  image: python:3.10
  script:
    - pip install env-doctor
    - env-doctor check --ci
  rules:
    - if: $CI_PIPELINE_SOURCE == "merge_request_event"

docs.title: JSON Output
docs.text:
For custom parsing and reporting:

env-doctor check --json

docs.title: Parsing in Bash
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash

# Run check and capture output
RESULT=$(env-doctor check --json)

# Extract specific fields with jq
STATUS=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.status')
DRIVER_VERSION=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.checks.driver.version')
CUDA_VERSION=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.checks.cuda.version')

echo "Status: $STATUS"
echo "Driver: $DRIVER_VERSION"
echo "CUDA: $CUDA_VERSION"

# Conditional logic
if [ "$STATUS" = "error" ]; then
  echo "Environment check failed!"
  exit 1
fi

docs.title: Parsing in Python
docs.text:
import json
import subprocess

result = subprocess.run(
    ["env-doctor", "check", "--json"],
    capture_output=True,
    text=True
)
data = json.loads(result.stdout)

# Access check results
if data["status"] == "success":
    print(f"Driver: {data['checks']['driver']['version']}")
    print(f"CUDA: {data['checks']['cuda']['version']}")
else:
    print(f"Issues found: {data['summary']['issues_count']}")

docs.title: Conditional Installation
docs.text:
Install GPU or CPU packages based on environment:

#!/bin/bash

# Check if GPU is available
if env-doctor check --json | jq -e '.checks.driver.detected' > /dev/null; then
  echo "GPU detected, installing CUDA version"
  pip install torch torchvision
else
  echo "No GPU, installing CPU version"
  pip install torch torchvision --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu
fi

docs.title: Pre-commit Hook
docs.text:
Validate Dockerfiles before commit:

# .pre-commit-config.yaml
repos:
  - repo: local
    hooks:
      - id: validate-dockerfile
        name: Validate Dockerfile
        entry: env-doctor dockerfile --ci
        language: system
        files: Dockerfile
        pass_filenames: false

docs.title: Monitoring Integration
docs.text:
Store results for tracking over time:

import json
import subprocess
from datetime import datetime

def collect_env_metrics():
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["env-doctor", "check", "--json"],
        capture_output=True,
        text=True
    )
    data = json.loads(result.stdout)

    return {
        "timestamp": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        "status": data["status"],
        "driver_version": data["checks"]["driver"].get("version"),
        "cuda_version": data["checks"]["cuda"].get("version"),
        "issues_count": data["summary"]["issues_count"]
    }

# Send to your monitoring system
metrics = collect_env_metrics()
# monitoring_client.send(metrics)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• check Command - Full command reference
  • GitHub Actions Example

docs.title: Recording Demos
docs.text: This guide explains how to record terminal demos for the documentation.

docs.title: Asciinema (Recommended)
docs.text: Asciinema creates lightweight, copy-pasteable terminal recordings.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
# macOS
brew install asciinema

# Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt install asciinema

# pip
pip install asciinema

docs.title: Recording
docs.text:
# Start recording
asciinema rec demo.cast

# Run your commands...
env-doctor check
env-doctor model llama-3-8b

# Press Ctrl+D or type 'exit' to stop

docs.title: Tips for Good Recordings
docs.text:
1. Clear screen first: Start with clear
  2. Type slowly: Pause between commands for readability
  3. Keep it short: 15-30 seconds is ideal
  4. Show one feature: Focus on a single command per recording

docs.title: Uploading
docs.text:
# Upload to asciinema.org
asciinema upload demo.cast

# Or authenticate first for your account
asciinema auth
asciinema upload demo.cast

docs.title: Embedding in Docs
docs.text:
After uploading, you'll get a URL like https://asciinema.org/a/123456.

In Markdown:

[![asciicast](https://asciinema.org/a/123456.svg)](https://asciinema.org/a/123456)

As JavaScript embed (auto-plays):

<script src="https://asciinema.org/a/123456.js" id="asciicast-123456" async data-autoplay="true" data-speed="1.5"></script>

docs.title: Recording Checklist
docs.text:
Before recording:

  • [ ] Clean terminal (no sensitive info)
  • [ ] Clear history if needed
  • [ ] Set consistent terminal size (80x24 recommended)
  • [ ] Prepare commands to run

Recording:

  • [ ] Clear screen
  • [ ] Type commands clearly
  • [ ] Pause to let output be readable
  • [ ] Keep under 30 seconds

After recording:

  • [ ] Review the recording
  • [ ] Re-record if needed
  • [ ] Upload and get embed code

docs.title: WSL2 GPU Support
docs.text: Env-Doctor provides comprehensive WSL2 environment detection and GPU forwarding validation.

docs.title: Environment Detection
docs.text:
Env-Doctor automatically detects your environment:

Environment GPU Support Native Linux Full CUDA support WSL1 No GPU support (CUDA not available) WSL2 GPU forwarding from Windows host

docs.title: How WSL2 GPU Works
docs.text:
In WSL2, GPU access is forwarded from the Windows host:

Windows Host (NVIDIA Driver) → WSL2 (CUDA libraries) → Your Application

Key points:

  • NVIDIA driver is installed on Windows only
  • WSL2 uses special libraries in /usr/lib/wsl/lib/
  • Do NOT install NVIDIA drivers inside WSL2

docs.title: WSL2 Validation
docs.text:
Run env-doctor check to validate your WSL2 GPU setup:

env-doctor check

Healthy WSL2 Output:

🩺 ENV-DOCTOR DIAGNOSIS
============================================================

🖥️  Environment: WSL2 (GPU forwarding enabled)

🎮 GPU Driver
   ✅ NVIDIA Driver: 535.146.02 (via WSL forwarding)
   └─ Max CUDA: 12.2

✅ GPU forwarding is working correctly!

docs.title: Driver Installed Inside WSL
docs.text:
Problem:

❌ NVIDIA driver installed inside WSL. This breaks GPU forwarding.

Cause: You installed nvidia-driver-* packages inside WSL2.

Fix:

sudo apt remove --purge nvidia-* libnvidia-*
sudo apt autoremove

Then restart WSL:

# In PowerShell (Windows)
wsl --shutdown

docs.title: Missing CUDA Libraries
docs.text:
Problem:

❌ Missing /usr/lib/wsl/lib/libcuda.so

Cause: Windows NVIDIA driver not properly installed or outdated.

Fix:

  1. Update Windows NVIDIA driver to 470.76 or newer
  2. Download from nvidia.com/drivers
  3. Restart Windows after installation

docs.title: nvidia-smi Not Working
docs.text:
Problem:

❌ nvidia-smi command failed

Possible causes:

  1. Windows driver too old - Update to 470.76+
  2. WSL version outdated - Run wsl --update
  3. Hyper-V not enabled - Required for WSL2 GPU

Fix:

# Update WSL
wsl --update

# Verify WSL2 version
wsl --version

docs.title: Running in WSL1
docs.text:
Problem:

⚠️  WSL1 detected. CUDA is not supported in WSL1.

Fix: Convert to WSL2:

# List distributions
wsl --list --verbose

# Convert to WSL2
wsl --set-version <distro-name> 2

docs.title: Windows Requirements
docs.text:
• Windows 10 version 21H2 or newer (or Windows 11)
  • NVIDIA driver 470.76 or newer
  • WSL2 (not WSL1)

docs.title: WSL2 Requirements
docs.text:
• Do NOT install NVIDIA drivers
  • CUDA toolkit installation is optional (most libraries bundle it)
  • cuDNN can be installed if needed

docs.title: Minimal Setup (Most Users)
docs.text:
1. Install NVIDIA driver on Windows
  2. Install WSL2 with Ubuntu
  3. Install Python packages normally
# In WSL2
pip install torch  # Works out of the box

docs.title: Development Setup (For Compilation)
docs.text:
If you need to compile CUDA extensions:

# Install CUDA toolkit (not driver!)
wget https://developer.download.nvidia.com/compute/cuda/repos/wsl-ubuntu/x86_64/cuda-keyring_1.1-1_all.deb
sudo dpkg -i cuda-keyring_1.1-1_all.deb
sudo apt update
sudo apt install cuda-toolkit-12-1

# Set environment
export CUDA_HOME=/usr/local/cuda-12.1
export PATH=$CUDA_HOME/bin:$PATH

docs.title: Check Windows Driver
docs.text:
In PowerShell:

nvidia-smi

Should show driver version 470.76+.

docs.title: Check WSL2 GPU Access
docs.text:
In WSL2:

nvidia-smi

Should show same driver as Windows.

docs.title: Check Library Paths
docs.text:
ls -la /usr/lib/wsl/lib/

Should contain libcuda.so, libnvidia-ml.so, etc.

docs.title: Full Debug Output
docs.text:
env-doctor debug

Shows detailed WSL2 detection metadata.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• check Command
  • cuda-info Command
